있다.english: (verb) to exist / to have / to be (at a location)
있다.trap: 있어요 NOT 있아요. 계세요 = honorific form.

없다.english: (verb) to not exist / to not have

되다.english: (verb) to become / to be okay / to work out
되다.trap: 돼요 = 되어요 (contracted). 되요 is a spelling error.

보다.english: (verb) to see / to watch / to try (~아/어 보다)

알다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 아는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ), 압니다 (before ㅂ), 아세요 (before 시). 알아요 (present). Negative = 모르다 (separate verb, not 안 알다).

일어나다.english: (verb) to wake up / to get up / to happen

걷다.trap: ㄷ-irregular: 걷→걸 before vowels. 걸어요 NOT 걷어요. 걸었어요, 걸을, 걸어서.

타다.english: (verb) to ride / to take (transport) / to burn

내리다.english: (verb) to get off / to come down / to fall (rain/snow)

듣다.trap: ㄷ-irregular: 듣→들 before vowels. 들어요 NOT 듣어요. 들었어요.

읽다.trap: 읽어요. Double batchim ㄺ: sounds like 일거요 in speech.

쓰다.english: (verb) to write / to use / to wear (hat/glasses) / (adj) bitter
쓰다.trap: 으-irregular: 쓰+어→써. 써요 NOT 쓰어요. 썼어요.

말하다.english: (verb) to speak / to say / to tell

이야기하다.english: (verb) to talk / to chat / to tell a story

묻다.trap: ㄷ-irregular: 묻→물 before vowels. 물어요 (to ask). Note: 묻다 meaning 'to bury' is regular (묻어요).

주다.trap: 줘요 = 주+어요 (contracted). 드리다 = humble form (giving to elders/seniors).

드리다.trap: 드려요 = 드리+어요. Use instead of 주다 when giving to elders.

팔다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 파는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ), 팝니다 (before ㅂ). 팔아요 (present).

열다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 여는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ). 열어요 (present).

찾다.english: (verb) to look for / to find / to withdraw (money)

헤어지다.english: (verb) to separate / to break up / to part ways

살다.english: (verb) to live / to reside / to be alive
살다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 사는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ), 삽니다 (before ㅂ), 사세요 (before 시). 살아요 (present).

만들다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 만드는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ). 만들어요 (present).

놀다.english: (verb) to play / to hang out / to be idle

돕다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 돕+아→도와요 (not 도봐요 or 도워요). 도왔어요 (past).

쉬다.english: (verb) to rest / to take a break

오르다.english: (verb) to go up / to rise / to climb

좋아하다.english: (verb) to like / to be fond of
좋아하다.trap: Contrast with 좋다 (adjective = to be good). 좋아하다 expresses liking something.

시작하다.trap: 시작하다 (you start something, transitive) vs 시작되다 (something starts, intransitive).

끝나다.english: (verb) to end / to finish (intransitive — the thing ends by itself)
끝나다.trap: 수업이 끝나요 (class ends) vs 수업을 끝내요 (I finish class). 끝나다 = intransitive.

끝내다.english: (verb) to finish / to end (transitive — you end it)

결혼하다.trap: 결혼하다 — with: ~와/과 결혼하다 or ~하고 결혼하다 (marry WITH someone). Not 에게 결혼하다.

들어오다.english: (verb) to come in / to enter (toward speaker)

돌아가다.english: (verb) to go back / to return (away from speaker)

지나가다.english: (verb) to pass by / to go past

다니다.english: (verb) to attend / to commute / to go regularly
다니다.trap: 다녀요 = 다니+어요. 학교에 다녀요 = I attend school.

받아오다.english: (verb) to bring back / to go get

고치다.english: (verb) to fix / to repair / to correct

바꾸다.english: (verb) to change / to exchange / to switch

잡다.english: (verb) to grab / to catch / to hold

놓다.english: (verb) to put down / to let go / to place
놓다.trap: 놓아요 → 놔요 (contracted in speech). 놓았어요 → 놨어요.

넣다.trap: 넣어요. ㅎ batchim before 어: sounds like 너허요 → 너어요.

꺼내다.english: (verb) to take out / to pull out

켜다.trap: 켜요 = 켜+어요 (contracted). 껐어요 (past of 끄다, not 켜다).

열리다.english: (verb) to open (intransitive — door opens by itself)

닫히다.english: (verb) to close (intransitive — door closes by itself)

좋다.trap: 좋아요. 좋다 = 'to be good' (adjective). 좋아하다 = 'to like' (verb).

많다.trap: 많아요. ㄶ double batchim: before vowel, ㅎ drops — 많아요 sounds like 마나요.

높다.trap: 높아요. ㅍ batchim before ㅇ: 높아요 sounds like 노파요.

길다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 긴 (modifier, ㄹ drops before ㄴ). 길어요 (present).

짧다.trap: 짧아요. Double batchim ㄼ → before vowel: ㄹ sound. 짧아요 sounds like 짤바요.

넓다.trap: 넓어요. Double batchim ㄼ → before vowel: ㄹ sounds. 넓어요 sounds like 널버요.

행복하다.trap: 행복해요. 행복하다 = state of happiness (adjective). 기쁘다 = feeling joyful (momentary emotion).

화나다.english: (adjective/verb) to be angry / to get angry

무섭다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 무섭+어→무서워. 무서워요 (present). Can mean both 'it is scary' and 'I am scared'.

힘들다.english: (adjective) hard / tough / exhausting / difficult (emotionally or physically)

맛있다.trap: 맛있어요. Pronounced 마싯어요 (ㅅ batchim links to ㅇ).

뜨겁다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 뜨겁+어→뜨거워. 뜨거워요. Contrast: 덥다 = hot weather.

차갑다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 차갑+아→차가워. 차가워요. Contrast: 춥다 = cold weather.

시원하다.trap: 시원해요. Used for weather, drinks, and also emotional relief ('that's satisfying').

덥다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 덥+어→더워. 더워요 (present). Contrast: 뜨겁다 = hot to the touch.

춥다.trap: ㅂ-irregular: 춥+어→추워. 추워요 (present). Contrast: 차갑다 = cold to the touch.

밝다.trap: 밝아요. Double batchim ㄺ before ㅇ: sounds like 발가요.

멀다.trap: ㄹ-irregular: 먼 (modifier, ㄹ drops before ㄴ), 멉니다 (formal). 멀어요 (present).

젊다.trap: 젊어요. Double batchim ㄼ: sounds like 절머요.

늙다.trap: 늙어요. Use 오래되다 for old objects.

이다.trap: 이에요 after consonant; 예요 after vowel. 학생이에요 (student). 의사예요 (doctor). Past: 이었어요/였어요.

아니다.english: (copula) to not be — negates noun equation
아니다.trap: 아니에요 (it is not). N이/가 아니에요 = 'it is not N'. Past: 아니었어요.

나.trap: 나 = casual. 저 = formal/polite. 나의 → 내 (contracted possessive).

저.trap: 저 = polite. 나 = casual. 저의 → 제 (contracted possessive).

우리.english: (pronoun) we / our / my (used possessively in Korean — '우리 엄마' = 'my mom')
우리.trap: 우리 = 'we' but also used as a possessive 'my' in Korean culture. '우리 집' = 'my home / our home'.

너.trap: 너 = casual. 당신 is rarely used in speech; usually use the person's name or title. 너의 → 네 (but often 니 to avoid confusion with 네=yes).

당신.trap: 당신 is used in writing or between married couples. In speech, use the person's name, 선생님, 씨, etc. instead.

이것.trap: 이것 → 이게 (subject), 이걸 (object) in speech. 이거 (casual spoken form).

그것.english: (pronoun) that (thing) — close to listener / previously mentioned
그것.trap: 그것 → 그게, 그걸, 그거 in speech.

저것.english: (pronoun) that (thing) — far from both speaker and listener
저것.trap: 저것 → 저게, 저걸, 저거 in speech.

저거.english: (pronoun) that over there (casual spoken form of 저것)

거기.english: (pronoun/adverb) there — near listener or previously mentioned place

저기.trap: 저기요 = polite way to get someone's attention ('excuse me').

이.english: (determiner) this — used before a noun (이 책 = this book)

그.english: (determiner) that — used before a noun (그 사람 = that person)

저.english: (determiner) that over there — used before a noun (저 건물 = that building over there)
저.trap: Note: different from 저 (I/me). Context distinguishes them.

형.trap: 형 = male speaker's older brother. 오빠 = female speaker's older brother. 오빠 also used for older male friend by females.

오빠.english: (noun) older brother (used by females) / older male friend (female usage)

언니.trap: 언니 = female speaker's older sister. 누나 = male speaker's older sister.

동생.trap: 남동생 = younger brother. 여동생 = younger sister. 동생 alone = gender-neutral.

아내.trap: 부인 = wife (more formal). 와이프 is also used colloquially.

친구.trap: 친구 implies roughly same age. Use 선배/후배 for older/younger acquaintances in Korean social context.

선생님.english: (noun) teacher / honorific title for a respected person
선생님.trap: 선생님 = teacher, also used to address doctors, lawyers, and other respected figures.

머리.trap: 머리 = both 'head' and 'hair'. 머리가 아파요 = I have a headache. 머리를 감아요 = I wash my hair.

눈.trap: 눈 = eye AND snow — context distinguishes. 눈이 와요 = snow falls. 눈이 아파요 = eyes hurt.

팔.trap: 팔 = arm. Homophone: 팔 = 8 (native Korean number). Context distinguishes.

배.english: (noun) stomach / abdomen / belly / pear / boat / ship
배.trap: 배 has multiple meanings — all context-dependent.

등.trap: 등 = back (body). Also: 등 (lamp/light in compounds like 신호등).

다리.trap: 다리 = leg (body) AND bridge. 다리가 아파요 = legs hurt. 다리를 건너요 = cross a bridge.

이.trap: 이 = tooth (noun). Also the subject particle after consonants (이/가), and the demonstrative 'this'. Context is key.

시간.trap: 시간 = time in general or 'one hour' (한 시간). 시 = o'clock (두 시 = 2 o'clock).

이제.english: (adverb) now / from now on (implies a change)
이제.trap: 지금 = this moment. 이제 = 'now' with a sense of transition: '이제 가요' = 'I'm leaving now (finally/as of now)'.

밤.trap: 밤 = night AND chestnut (the nut). Context distinguishes.

낮.trap: 낮 vs 밤: 낮 = daytime, 밤 = nighttime.

날.trap: 날 = day (informal). 날이 좋아요 = the weather is nice.

주.trap: 이번 주 = this week. 다음 주 = next week. 지난 주 = last week.

년.trap: 올해 = this year. 내년 = next year. 작년 = last year.

밥.trap: 밥 = cooked rice AND 'a meal' in general. 밥 먹었어요? = Did you eat (have a meal)?

비빔밥.english: (noun) bibimbap (mixed rice bowl with vegetables and egg)

라면.trap: 라면 = Korean instant noodles (라면). Different from Japanese ramen in style.

사과.trap: 사과 = apple AND 'apology'. 사과해요 = I apologize. Context distinguishes.

배.trap: Same spelling as 배 (stomach/boat) — context distinguishes.

고기.trap: 소고기 = beef. 돼지고기 = pork. 닭고기 = chicken.

야채.trap: 야채 and 채소 are both used — 야채 is more colloquial.

차.trap: 차 = tea (녹차 = green tea) AND car/vehicle. Context distinguishes.

옷.trap: 옷 pronounced 옫 — ㅅ batchim sounds like ㄷ in isolation.

시계.trap: 손목시계 = wristwatch. 시계 alone = clock or watch.

집.trap: 우리 집 = my home (Koreans often say 'our' for possessions shared with family).

부엌.trap: 주방 is also common (more modern term).

화장실.trap: 화장실 = restroom (polite). 욕실 = bathroom with tub/shower.

아파트.trap: 아파트 = Korean apartments are typically high-rise. 빌라 = low-rise apartment building.

학교.trap: 초등학교 = elementary. 중학교 = middle. 고등학교 = high school. 대학교 = university.

시장.trap: 재래시장 = traditional market. 시장 also means 'mayor' — context distinguishes.

역.trap: 지하철역 = subway station. 기차역 = train station.

자동차.trap: 차 is the colloquial short form.

배.trap: 배 = boat, also = stomach, pear. Context distinguishes.

비.trap: 비가 와요 = it rains. 비가 와요 NOT 비가 내려요 (though both work, 와요 is standard).

눈.trap: 눈 = snow AND eye. 눈이 와요 = it snows.

바람.trap: 바람이 불어요 = the wind blows. 바람을 피우다 = to have an affair.

해.trap: 해가 떠요 = the sun rises. 올해 = this year. 해 = sun in everyday speech.

달.trap: 달이 떠요 = the moon rises. 이 달 = this month.

풀.trap: 풀 = grass AND glue — context distinguishes.

빨간색.trap: 빨간 (modifier before noun): 빨간 사과 = red apple.

초록색.trap: 녹색 is also used. 초록 is the everyday word.

아프다.english: (adjective/verb) to be sick / to hurt / to be in pain
아프다.trap: 아파요 = I'm sick / it hurts. 어디가 아파요? = Where does it hurt? Literally 'where is sick?'

약.trap: 약을 먹다 = to take medicine (lit. eat medicine).

감기.trap: 감기에 걸렸어요 = I caught a cold.

열.trap: 열이 나요 = I have a fever.

두통.trap: 머리가 아파요 is more commonly used in speech.

일.trap: 일 = work, day (1), or 'matter'. Context-dependent: 일이 많아요 = lots of work. 일 일 = day 1.

수업.trap: 수업에 가다 = go to class. 수업이 있다 = there is class.

시험.trap: 시험을 보다 = to take an exam.

이름.trap: 이름이 뭐예요? = What is your name?

나이.trap: 나이가 어떻게 돼요? = How old are you? Korean age system: add 1-2 years to Western age.

핸드폰.trap: 스마트폰 is also used. 핸드폰 is very common in everyday speech.

사진.trap: 사진을 찍다 = to take a photo.

영화.trap: 영화를 보다 = to watch a movie.

노래.trap: 노래를 부르다 = to sing a song.

여행.trap: 여행을 가다 = to go on a trip.

선물.trap: 선물을 주다 = to give a gift.

날.trap: 날 = day (informal) OR weather: 날이 좋아요 = the weather is nice.

생활.trap: 일상 = everyday life. 생활 = the act of living/lifestyle.

삶.english: (noun) life (abstract — the experience of being alive)
삶.trap: 삶 = life (abstract/philosophical). 생활 = daily life. 인생 = one's life as a journey.

인생.english: (noun) life (one's lifetime / life as a whole)

꿈.trap: 꿈을 꾸다 = to dream. 꿈이 있어요 = I have a dream (ambition).

기억.trap: 기억하다 = to remember. 기억이 나다 = to recall.

마음.trap: 마음이 따뜻하다 = warm-hearted. 마음을 먹다 = to make up one's mind.

기분.trap: 기분이 좋아요 = I feel good. 기분이 나빠요 = I feel bad.

희망.trap: 희망하다 = to hope (verb). 바라다 = to wish/hope.

걱정.trap: 걱정하다 = to worry. 걱정하지 마세요 = Don't worry.

생각.trap: 생각하다 = to think (verb). 생각이 있다 = to have a thought/idea.

빨리.trap: 빨리 = quickly. 빠르다 = to be fast (adjective). 빨리 와요 = Come quickly!

함께.trap: 같이 = together (more casual). 함께 = together (slightly formal but common).

같이.trap: 같이 = together (casual). Also means 'like/as': 나처럼/같이 = like me.

매우.trap: 아주 and 매우 are interchangeable; 매우 is slightly more formal.

너무.english: (adverb) too / very (often 'too much', but used like 'very' in casual speech)
너무.trap: 너무 technically means 'excessively', but Koreans use it as 'very' in everyday speech. 너무 좋아요 = I love it so much!

참.english: (adverb) truly / indeed / really / by the way
참.trap: 참 = 'truly/really' (literary). Also used as 'Oh, by the way': 참, 있잖아... = 'Oh, you know...'

좀.trap: 좀 = a little, but also softens requests: 좀 도와주세요 = Please help me (softer than 도와주세요 alone).

조금.trap: 조금 = a little (more neutral than 좀). 조금만요 = just a little.

많이.trap: 많이 먹어요 = Eat a lot. 많이 피곤해요 = I'm very tired.

이미.trap: 이미 = already (done, in the past). 벌써 = already (sooner than expected).

벌써.trap: 벌써 = already (with surprise). 이미 = already (neutral, factual).

아직.trap: 아직 = still (ongoing) or 'not yet'. 아직 몰라요 = I still don't know. 아직 안 왔어요 = hasn't come yet.

드디어.trap: 드디어 왔어요 = Finally came! Implies long anticipation.

또.trap: 또 와요 = Come again. 또는 = or (conjunction).

더.trap: 더 주세요 = Give me more please.

항상.trap: 항상 = always. 언제나 = always/every time (slightly stronger).

보통.trap: 보통 = usually. 보통이다 = to be ordinary/normal.

사실.trap: 사실 = actually (adverb) OR truth/fact (noun). 사실은... = Actually...

뭐.trap: 뭐 = casual form of 무엇. 뭐예요? = What is it?

누구.trap: 누가 왔어요? = Who came? (누가 = 누구+이/가 subject form)

어디.trap: 어디서 = from where / at where (어디+에서). 어디에 = to where.

어떻게.trap: 어떻게 됐어요? = How did it turn out?

얼마나.english: (question word) how much / how many / to what extent
얼마나.trap: 얼마나 걸려요? = How long does it take?

몇.trap: 몇 시예요? = What time is it? 몇 명? = How many people?

그런데.trap: 그런데 = 'however' (contrast) or 'by the way' (topic shift). Often 근데 in speech.

그러나.trap: 하지만 and 그렇지만 are more colloquial equivalents.

그래도.trap: 그래도 괜찮아요 = It's still okay / Even so, it's fine.

그래요.english: (expression) yes / that's right / I see / okay
그래요.trap: 그래요 = 'Yes/right' (agreeing). 그래요? = 'Is that so?' (question). 그렇군요 = 'I see' (realizing).
